coredata.dc:title: Electrochemical luminescence sensor based on CDs@HKUST-1 composite for detection of catechol
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Lijun
Shan, Xueling
Jiang, Ding
Wang, Wenchang
Chen, Zhidong
coredata.dc:description: Catechol (CC) exists a potential threat to human psychology and physiology. In this research, a novel ultra-sensitive electrochemiluminescence (ECL) sensor was established to detect CC based on carbon dots (CDs) and metal organic framework (HKUST-1) synergy system. The results showed good large specific surface area of HKUST-1 on CDs which could effectively improve the sensitivity of sensor. Under the optimal conditions, the as-fabricated sensor illustrated a wide linear range of 5.0 × 10−9–2.5 × 10−5 mol/L with the detection limit of 3.8 × 10−9 mol/L(S/N = 3). In addition, owning to its good reproducibility and stability, the sensor had been successfully applied to the actual sample. This work provides an important reference for the application of metal organic framework materials (MOFs) in ECL researches and a novel method for detecting catechol.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemiluminescence
Carbon dots
HKUST-1
Catechol

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrochemical characterization of the proposed sensor

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of the Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions of China ( 18KJB150001 and 19KJB150003 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province ( BK20190928 ), the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51874050 and 21904014 ) and the Foundation of Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Advanced Catalytic Materials and Technology ( BM2012110 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM of HKUST-1 (A); SEM of CDs@HKUST-1 (B); Nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherm of the CDs@HKSUT-1(C); XRD pattern of CDs (D); XRD pattern of HKUST-1(a) and CDs@HKUST-1(b) (E);FT-TR spectrum of HKUST-1(a), CDs@HKUST-1(b) (F).

CV curves (A) and EIS (B) of bare GCE(a), CDs/GCE(b), HKUST-1/GCE(c), and CDs@HKUST-1/GCE(d) in 0.1 mol/L KCl and 10 mmol/L Fe (CN) 6 3−/4− solutions. ECL-potential curves (C) of bare GCE (a), HKUST-1 (b), CDs(c), CDs@HKUST-1 (d),CDs@HKUST-1/CC(e) in 0.1 mol/L PBS (pH 7.4) containing 50 mmol/L K2S2O8 with scan rate of 100 mV/s;The concentration of CC was 2.5 × 10−7 mol/L.
ECL mechanism for CDs@HKUST-1 / K2S2O8 system.

Effect of CDs@HKUST-1 modification on ΔECL (A); effect of scan rate on ΔECL (B); effect of potassium persulfate concentration on ΔECL (C); effect of pH on ΔECL (D); ΔIECL = I0-I, I0 and I are the ECL intensities of CDs@HKUST-1/GCE in the absence and presence of 5.0 × 10−6 mol/L catechol, respectively. Photomultiplier tube (PMT) set to 800 V.

ECL-E curve (A) and corresponding linear relationship (B) of different concentrations of catechol; ECL signals (C) of CDs@HKUST-1 monitored during 20 cycles. The ECL intensity responses of the 5.0 × 10−6 mol/L CC and CC with different concentrations of interferences (D). Error bars = RSD (n = 3).PBS: 0.1 mol/L pH 7.4; K2S2O8: 0.05 mol/L; Potential range: -1.6– 0 V; Sweep rate: 0.1 V/ s; PMT: 800 V. catechol concentration: (a) 5 × 10−9, (b) 2.5 × 10−8, (c) 5.0 × 10−8, (d) 2.5 × 10−7, (e) 5.0 × 10 −7, (f) 5.0 × 10−6, (g) 2.5 × 10−5 mol / L.
Comparison of the method and the reported method for detecting catechol.

head.ce:title: Electrochemical luminescence sensor based on CDs@HKUST-1 composite for detection of catechol

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Advanced Catalytic Materials and Technology, School of Petrochemical Engineering, Changzhou University, Changzhou 213164, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Advanced Catalytic Materials and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advanced Catalysis and Green Manufacturing Collaborative Innovation Center， Changzhou University, Changzhou 213164, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Advanced Catalysis and Green Manufacturing Collaborative Innovation Center

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Advanced Catalysis and Green Manufacturing Collaborative Innovation Center，Changzhou University, Changzhou 213164, China.

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Advanced Catalytic Materials and Technology, School of Petrochemical Engineering, Changzhou University, Changzhou 213164, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Advanced Catalytic Materials and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Catechol (CC) exists a potential threat to human psychology and physiology. In this research, a novel ultra-sensitive electrochemiluminescence (ECL) sensor was established to detect CC based on carbon dots (CDs) and metal organic framework (HKUST-1) synergy system. The results showed good large specific surface area of HKUST-1 on CDs which could effectively improve the sensitivity of sensor. Under the optimal conditions, the as-fabricated sensor illustrated a wide linear range of 5.0 × 10−9–2.5 × 10−5 mol/L with the detection limit of 3.8 × 10−9 mol/L(S/N = 3). In addition, owning to its good reproducibility and stability, the sensor had been successfully applied to the actual sample. This work provides an important reference for the application of metal organic framework materials (MOFs) in ECL researches and a novel method for detecting catechol.

ce:section.ce:para: Catechol, a typical phenolic compound, as a stabilizing additive [1,2], was widely used in the synthesis of foods, pharmaceuticals and agrochemicals. In nature, it was widely found in tea, vegetables and fruits [3]. Studies have shown that the intake of appropriate amounts of catechol had antioxidant and antiviral effects, but it was easy to induce cancer in human tissues when ingested excessively [4]. Therefore, the accurate detection of catechol content in tea by scientific means had far-reaching significance for tea quality and people's health. At present, many methods have been used to detect catechol, such as, spectrophotometry [5], electrochemical method [6], gas chromatography [7], and high performances liquid chromatography (HPLC) [8]. However, these methods had a certain degree of deficiencies, such as complex operation, high cost and wasting time. Therefore, it was of great significance to study a low-cost, sensitive and rapid method which can accurately and quantitatively detect catechol.

Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) is a new analytical method that continues and combines electrochemistry and spectroscopy [9,10]. Traditional classic illuminators, such as triple pyridinium Ru (bpy)3 2+ and luminol [11,12] were expensive, poor stability, highly wasteful, and not independent. In order to solve the defects of classical illuminators, and further enhance the performances and applications of different materials in the sensing field, people began to focus on the development of quantum dot (QDs) materials. So far, QDs have achieved many satisfactory results in ECL biosensing applications [13–16]. In which, owning to its advantages, such as, non-toxic, high quantum yield, fluorescence control, and biological phase, carbon dots (CDs) nanomaterials have become research hotspots and gradually introduced into ECL systems [17,18]. However, the low luminescence strength of CDs has hindered the progress of ECL sensor based on CDs system. Recently, more and more different materials have been hybridized with CDs for signal amplification. To enhance the ECL intensity efficiently, Chi's group prepared a new ultrasensitive ECL sensor with carbon dot capped gold nanoflower (CD/Au NF) showed good selectivity towards thrombin [19]. Deng's team constructed a sandwich ECL sensor based on a multiple intensity amplification strategy with functionalized SiO2@CQDs/Au NPs for the analysis of alpha-fetoprotein [20]. It's crucial to exploit much more novel ECL sensor based on CDs complex.

MOFs are inorganic-organic solid compounds formed by metal ions and organic linkers, widely used in catalysis, molecular separation, sensing and biomedical diagnosis [21] because of their controllable size, large specific surface area, high porosity and easy surface functionalization. HKUST-1 is a copper-based metal−organic framework, Chui et al. [22] first reported copper-based MOFs (HKUST-1) with good catalytic activity [23], compared with MIL-101 (Fe) and ZIF-8 (Zn), HKUST-1 was more hydrophilic [24], so we chose copper-based MOFs (HKUST-1) as a carrier for the preparation of composites. Additionally, MOFs had porous and regular structure which were beneficial to load much more quantum dots (QDs), so that increased the response of ECL sensor. For example, You et al. took advantage of the excellent catalytic sites and large specific area of MOFs to load with RGO-CdTe QDs. The complex endowed the ECL sensor with improved sensitivity for clenbuterol detection [25]. Furthermore, the poor properties of QDs were enhanced by the presence of other materials, so that we can design an electrochemical analysis method based on HKUST-1 with surface modification and excellent performance to load environmentally friendly luminescent nanomaterials CDs for catechol detection.

In this study, CDs@HKUST-1 composites were prepared by one-pot hydrothermal method. The composite was modified on glassy carbon electrode (GCE) for ECL analysis. Experimental results showed that the participation of HKUST-1 significantly enhanced the ECL signal of CDs, thus improving the sensitivity of analysis. When trace CC was present, the ECL signal of CDs@HKUST-1 was obviously quenched. On this basis, a sensitive detection model for catechol was established and applied to actual detection of catechol in tea.

Copper nitrate trihydrate (Cu(NO3)2·3H2O), trimesic acid (C9H6O6) were purchased from Aladdin; sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4·2H2O), disodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4·12H2O) were purchased from Shanghai Lingfeng Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Hydrochloric acid (HCl), methanol (CH4O), absolute ethanol (C2H6O), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), N,N-dimethylformamide (C3H7NO), citric acid (C6H8O7), ethylenediamine (C2H8N2), potassium persulfate (K2S2O8) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. The tea (coded as GT1) was purchased from Changzhou local supermarket. All drugs were analytical grades and the solutions were prepared in ultrapure water (>18 MΩ).

The scanning electron microscope (SEM) were received by a SEM instrument (JSM-6360LA, Japanese). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) characterization was analyzed by a Nicolet In10 (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Electrochemical characterizations including cyclic voltammetry (CV) and Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were studied by CHI660D electrochemical workstation (Shanghai CH Instrument Co., Ltd., China) and ZENNIUM electrochemical workstation (Zahner Instruments, Germany), respectively. ECL measurements were performed on MPI-B multifunctional ECL system (Xi'an Ruimai Analytical Instrument Co., Ltd.), with three-electrode system, which chose glassy carbon electrode (GCE) as a working electrode, platinum wire as a counter electrode and Ag/AgCl as a reference electrode for ECL detection. Xray diffraction (XRD) was carried out with a D/Max-2000 system (Rigaku, Japan). The Bruner-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area was tested by an ASAP 2000 (Micromeritics, America) gas sorption analyzer at 77 K.

The preparation method of carbon dots was referred to the reported literature [26] and the synthesis method was slightly modified. In short, accurately weighed 1.0512 g of citric acid, 0.34 mL of ethylenediamine, dissolved in 10 mL of ultrapure water, and stirred for 10 min to form a homogeneous and transparent solution, then transferred into 50 mL of PTFE autoclave, subsequently the reaction was carried out at 200 °C for 5 h. After the reaction was completed, the mixture was cooled to room temperature, and the liquid in the autoclave was transferred to a dialysis membrane and dialyzed in ultrapure water. Finally, the liquid in the membrane was centrifuged and dried, a black carbon powder was obtained.

CDs@HKUST-1 composite was prepared by one-pot hydrothermal synthesis. In brief, the mixture solution of 0.145 mol/L copper nitrate trihydrate aqueous solution and 0.083 mol/L isophthalic acid ethanol solution was magnetically stirred for 30 min to form a uniformly mixed HKUST-1 precursor solution [27]. 20 mg of the obtained carbon powder was added into the precursor solution of HKUST-1, and then stirred for 1 h at room temperature. The solution was then transferred to a 50 mL Teflon autoclave at 120 °C for another 12 h. After the reaction was completed, the mixture was cooled to room temperature, and the liquid in the autoclave was centrifuged and dried to obtain a green powdery substance CDs@HKUST-1. Finally, 1.0 mg of CDs@HKUST-1 powder was weighed and made up to a 1 mg/mL dispersion with N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) and stored for later use.

The glassy carbon electrode (GCE, Φ = 3 mm) was sequentially polished on the suede with 0.50 μm, 0.30 μm and 0.05 μm Al2O3, and then ultrasonicated with aqueous nitric acid solution, aqueous ethanol solution and ultrapure water for 20 s. The microinjector was pipetted with 3 μg of 1 mg/mL CDs@HKUST-1 DMF dispersion onto the surface of glassy carbon electrode (GCE), and dried to obtain CDs@HKUST-1/GCE. The ECL sensor was constructed using a three-electrode system with a CDs@HKUST-1 modified glassy carbon electrode as the working electrode, an Ag/AgCl electrode as the reference electrode, and a platinum electrode as the auxiliary electrode. We used a series of different concentrations of catechol standard solution for the ECL testes. Blank control experiment was performed by directly placing the three electrodes in a 0.1 mol/L phosphate buffer (PBS) containing 0.05 mol/L potassium persulfate at pH = 7.4 without catechol. The entire ECL cyclic voltammetric scanning process was carried out in the electrochemical window range of −1.6 to 0 V, the photomultiplier tube high voltage of 800 V, the sweep speed of 0.1 V/s, and the E-ECL curve was recorded at the same time. The determination was based on the difference in ECL intensity (ΔIECL), ΔIECL = I 0-I, where I0 was the electrochemiluminescence intensity of CDs@HKUST-1/GCE at different concentrations of catechol and I was the electrochemiluminescence intensity of CDs@HKUST-1/GCE without catechol. Since the logarithm of the concentration of ΔECL and catechol has a linear relationship, the concentration of catechol can be obtained by scientific calculation. The whole fabrication process of the ECL sensor was depicted as Fig. S1.

First of all, the tea sample was ground with a mortar, then 1.0 g of the ground tea powder was added to 60 mL of 80% methanol aqueous solution, subsequently the solution was extracted and filtered through a 0.22 μm filter. Finally a filtrate containing 0.05 mol/L K2S2O8 and 0.1 mol/L PBS (pH 7.4) was prepared and diluted to 100 mL, and 20.0 mL of the solution was taken in a detection cell for electrochemiluminescence test.

Size and morphology of HKUST-1 and CDs@HKSUT-1 were characterized by SEM. Fig. 1A showed that HKUST-1 was a single regular octahedral structure [27] which may have high specific surface area. The morphology of the copper-based metal organic framework (CDs@HKUST-1) doped with carbon dots can be clearly seen from Fig. 1B, indicating that carbon dots were successfully compounded on the surface of metal organic framework material (HKUST-1). The TEM of CDs were depicted in supporting information (Fig. S2), the nanoparticles about 10 nm can be easily observed. The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm and pore size distribution (inset) of CDs@HKUST-1 and HKUST-1 were investigated in Fig. 1C and S3, respectively. The average pore size of HKUST-1 alone was 7.937 nm and the surface area was 891.8 m2/g. After loading the carbon dots, the surface area of CDs@HKUST-1 rose to 1083.7 m2/g. Obviously the loading of carbon dots could provide more active sites and mass transfer channels. The adsorption desorption isotherm had a wide adsorption hysteresis loop between 0.5 and 1.0 (P/P0), indicating that some pores of HKUST-1 were blocked by carbon dots, which further demonstrated that the CDs was successfully loaded on the surface of HKUST-1.

The structure of HKUST-1 and CDs@HKSUT-1 were further characterized by XRD. Fig. 1D and E presented the XRD spectra of CDs, HKUST-1 and CDs@HKUST-1, respectively. From the XRD spectrum of carbon dots, there was a wide peak centered at 25° and the interlayer distance was calculated by Bragg's law was about 3.4 Å, which can be attributed to the existence of disordered carbon atoms [26]. The XRD spectrum of HKUST-1 (curve a) showed multiple diffraction peaks at 2θ = 6.7°, 9.4°, 11.8°, 13.3°, 14.6°, 16.6°, 17.5° and 19.1°, which was consistent with the diffraction peak positions (200), (220), (222), (400), (331), (422), (333) and (440) in the literature [28,29]. The XRD spectrum of CDs@HKUST-1 (curve b) clearly showed all the absorption peaks of the two materials, indicating the successful preparation of the complex. After doping with carbon dots, the intensity of the diffraction peak of HKUST-1 was weakened. The trend of characteristic diffraction peaks was in well conformity to the reported literatures [26,30,31]. And there was no change at the position of the diffraction peak about HKUST-1, showing that the crystallinity of HKUST-1 didn't destroy after doping the CDs, which further demonstrated the successful combination of the two materials. Fig. 1F indicated the FT-IR spectra of HKUST-1 and CDs@HKUST-1. From the FT-IR spectrum of HKUST-1, the vibration peak of VCO and VC–O were at 1642 cm−1 and 1379 cm−1 respectively; the peak at 1447 cm−1 may be ascribed to OH bending vibration, other absorption peaks were observed at 1100 cm−1 and 730 cm−1, which represented stretching vibration absorption peak of C-O-Cu and CuO [32].There was no peak at 1720 cm−1 and 1680 cm−1, indicating that carboxylation deprotonation of HKUST-1 [27]. The FT-IR of CDs can be characterized by citing a synthesis literature [26]. The absorption bands were assigned to the stretching mode of OH and the presence of carbonyl (C=O). All these indicated that there were more active sites of copper ions, which can further improve the catalytic performance of HKUST-1. Moreover, no apparent change was found between HKUST-1 and CDs@HKUST-1, manifesting the introduction of CDs didn't destroy its structure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical characterization of the proposed sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was applied to investigate different modified electrodes. Fig. 2A showed bare glassy carbon electrodes (GCE), CDs/GCE, HKUST-1/GCE and CDs@HKUST-1/GCE in a 10 mmol/L [Fe (CN)6]3−/4− solution containing 0.1 mol/L KCl. It can be seen from Fig. 2A that a pair of obvious reversible redox peaks (curve a) could be seen on the bare GCE. After the CDs were modified, the peak current decreased slightly (curve b). While the HKUST-1 was dispensed on GCE, the two peak currents were significantly reduced and the peak potential significantly increased (curve c). This was attributed to the poor conductivity of metal organic framework material and it was difficult for [Fe (CN)6]3−/4 - to reach the electrode surface through the modification layer. As for CDs@HKUST-1 modified elect, there was a certain increase in peak current compared to HKUST-1 alone (curve d). These phenomena confirmed that the introduction of CDs could effectively improve the electron transfer ability and greatly enhance the electrochemical performance.

It's crucial to determine the electron transfer ability of the stepwise assembly process of the sensor, Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was investigated using [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− as a redox probe at the electrode interface [33]. (Fig. 2B). The equivalent circuit (Fig. 2B, inset) used for fitting the impedance data, where Rs, Ret, Wd and Q represented solution resistance, electron transfer resistance, weber impedance and constant phase elements, respectively. Fig. 2B was Nyquist figure of GCE, CDs/GCE, HKUST-1/GCE and CDs@HKUST-1/GCE in 0.1 mol/L KCl with 10 mmol/L Fe(CN)6 3−/4−solutions. The scan frequency was from 105 Hz to 0.1 Hz, and the data results were in well agreement with the equivalent circuit based on Z-view software. The calculated Ret values at 149.6 Ω, 712.8 Ω, 2946 Ω and 1933 Ω corresponding to bare GCE, CDs/GCE, HKUST-1/GCE and CDs@HKUST-1/GCE respectively. Among them, HKUST-1 showed the highest Ret value, indicating that the interface resistance of the electrode solution of HKUST-1 was the most significant. The Ret value of CDs@HKUST-1 was lower than that of HKUST-1, which indicated that the synergy between CDs and HKUST-1 effectively increased the electron transfer between electroactive ion Fe (CN)6 3−/4− and the electrode interface. The coupling of CDs and HKUST-1 could highly enhance the conductivity of the sensor.

In order to scrupulously explore the feasibility of the luminescent system, the luminescence behavior of different modified electrodes was recorded. The ECL behavior of bare GCE, HKUST-1/GCE, CDs/GCE, CDs@HKUST-1/GCE was tested in 0.1 mol/L PBS (pH 7.4) containing 0.05 mol/L K2S2O8 with a scan voltage of −1.6–0 V. As illustrated in Fig. 2C, there was almost no ECL signal could be observed on the bare GCE (curve a), and HKUST-1/GCE revealed a weaker ECL peak (curve b). When CDs were modified on bare GCE, the ECL signal intensity increased slightly, but the ECL intensity was not extremely high (curve c). Two materials presented simultaneously, the ECL signal of CDs@HKUST-1/GCE has been greatly improved (curve d), this phenomena showed that the incorporation of CDs on HKUST-1 could achieve synergistic effect and exhibit higher ECL response.

Further, due to the presence of HKUST-1 in sensor which might improve the sensitivity of detection. It is crucial to verify the as-prepared sensor exhibited higher sensitivity compared to pure CDs to detect CC, so CDs was served as pure luminophore to detection (Fig. S4). It could be seen that the ECL signals gradually decreased along with the increase of CC concentration from1 1.0× 10−6 to 1.0 × 10−3 mol/L (Fig. S4, a-g from top to bottom). To further affirm the existence of HKUST-1 achieved synergistic effect, improving the sensitivity of this sensor. We designed several comparative experiments, when CC (2.5 × 10−5 mol/L) was faced with two different modifications (Fig. S5, curve b), the ECL signals from CDs and CDs@HKUST-1 were recorded. For comparison, CDs@HKUST-1 showed significantly quenching effect than pure CDs. Together these performances suggested the synergy of CDs and HKUST-1 and CC had obvious quenching effect on luminescence intensity of this system. Based on this quenching phenomenon, a novel electrochemiluminescence mechanism for detecting CC was investigated.

According to reported literatures [34,35], we tentatively deduced the possible reaction routes which can be expressed as Eqs. (1)–(5). As illustrated in Fig. 3 , the sensor ECL behaviors were mainly caused by CDs@HKUST-1. First, CDs were reduced to CDs-• (Eq. (1)) and S2O8 2− which acted as a co-reactant will undergo a two-step reaction to reduce to SO4 -• (Eq. (2) and Eq. (3)) because of the applied negative potential. Then, CDs-• reacted with SO4 -• to generate free radical CDs* (Eq. (4)). Finally, the CDs* decayed back to the CDs, an ECL emission was produced (Eq. (5)). When the CC was present, the CDs* were consumed, reducing the ECL transmission signal.
(1)
CDs +
e −
→
CDs −
•
(2)
S 2
O 8
2 −
+
e −
→
S 2
O 8
2 −
•
(3)
S 2
O 8
2 −
• →
SO 4
2 −
+
SO 4
−
•
(4)
CDs −
• +
SO 4
− • → CDs ∗ +
SO 4
2 −
(5)
CDs ∗ → CDs + hν

To determine the effect of the experimental conditions on the ECL performances of the system. The modification amount CDs@HKUST-1, scanning rate, potassium persulfate (K2S2O8) concentration and buffer pH value on the quenching ECL luminescence intensity (ΔECL) have been recorded.

Fig. 4A investigated the effect of CDs@HKUST-1 amounts on the intensity of ΔECL. As present in Fig. 4A, the ΔECL intensity increases with the amount of complex modification increasing, and the ΔECL intensity reaches the maximum at 3 μg. This may be due to the fact that as the amount of CDs@HKUST-1 modification increases, more carbon dots were immobilized on the electrode surface, resulting in a higher ECL response. When the amount of complex modification exceeded 3 μg, the ΔECL signal begun to decrease. This might be assigned to that the excessive application of CDs@HKUST-1 on the electrode might increase the thickness of the surface film of the electrode, which caused emission scattering in the film, resulting in a decrease in ΔECL. Therefore, this experiment chose 3 μg as the optimal condition for the amount of modification.

Fig. 4B also investigated the effect of scan rate on ΔECL between 25 and 125 mV/s. As demonstrated in Fig. 4B, when the scan rate increased from 25 mV/s to 100 mV/s, the ΔECL intensity increased significantly, and while the scan rate was higher than 100 mV/s, the ΔECL decreased rapidly. Due to low scan rate was not conducive to the rapid generation of intermediates. As the scan rate increased, it would promote the generation of more CDs*, and ΔECL would show an upward trend. When the scan rate exceeded 100 mV/s, the rate of potassium persulfate consumption on the electrode surface was much higher than the diffusion rate from the solution to the electrode surface [36], resulting in a low instantaneous concentration of potassium persulfate near the electrode surface, accompanied by a decrease in ΔECL. Therefore, the scan rate of 100 mV/s was chosen as the optimal sweep speed for this experiment.

In the ECL detection, potassium persulfate was often used as a cathode co-reactant, so the influence of the concentration of potassium persulfate on the ECL intensity was investigated. The experiment examined effects of potassium persulfate with the concentration range of 10–70 mmol/L on ΔECL. As depicted in Fig. 4C, in the range of 10–50 mmol/L, the ΔECL intensity increased significantly with the increase of K2S2O8 concentration [37]. The ECL signal tended to be stable with the increase of the concentration of the co-reactant in the range of 50–70 mmol/L, which was caused by the limitation of the electrode surface [38]. Therefore, the K2S2O8 concentration was chosen to be 50 mmol/L as the optimal concentration for detection.

Under the optimal experimental conditions, different concentrations of catechol were added, and the ECL-potential curve was recorded, measurement results were shown in Fig. 5A (a–g from top to bottom). When the concentration of catechol was between 5 × 10−9–2.5 × 10−5 mol/L, the logarithm of ΔECL and catechol concentration had a good linear relationship. As confirmed by Fig. 5B, the linear regression equation was ΔECL =1574.6logC (μmol/L) + 5177.9 with a coefficient of 0.9953, and the detection limit was 3.8 × 10−9 mol/L (S/N = 3). In order to assess the performance of the sensor, the ECL detection method was compared with other methods for detecting catechol. The results were listed in Table 1 , which further demonstrated the wide linear range and low detection limit of the ECL sensor.

The stability of the ECL sensor was measured by continuous cyclic voltammetry in 0.1 mol/L PBS (pH 7.4) containing 0.05 mol/L K2S2O8, and evaluated system stability by performing an ECL-time curves of 20 cycles. The results showed in Fig. 5C, after continuous scanning for twenty laps, the change in electrochemiluminescence intensity value was small, the relative standard deviation (RSD) was 2.3%, indicating that the ECL system had excellent stability.

The as-fabricated sensor also studied the effect of the potential interfering substances, including catechins, glucose (Glu), ascorbic acid (AA), sodium and potassium salts [42],on the change in the ECL intensity. Under the condition of 0.1 mol/L PBS containing 5.0 × 10−6 mol/L catechol, as can be seen from Fig. 5D, when 2000-fold Na+, K+, 200-fold Glu and 500-fold AA were applied to the sensor, we obtained minimal change of ECL intensity. However, 10-fold catechin caused an evident decrease in the ECL signal, which may be attributed to that the substance can also be oxidized on the electrodes to become anthraquinones, so the method can also be applied to detect similar phenolic substances. Besides, the coexistence of these aforesaid interferences had an effect on the ECL intensity of <5%.

In order to evaluate the potential of this ECL sensor in actual detection, the standard recovery method was used to detect catechol in a brand of tea. 0.108 mg/g and 0.201 mg/g catechol were added to the actual samples, respectively, and the recovery experiments were performed three times in parallel at each concentration. The results are summarized in supporting information. (Table S1). The recovery of the samples at the two spiked concentrations were 103.7% and 98.1%, respectively, and the average relative standard deviations were 1.1% and 1.8%, indicating that the method was reliable and can be used for actual sample analysis of catechol.

In this work, the preparation method of CDs@HKUST-1 was first proposed, and a new ECL sensor based on CDs@HKUST-1/GCE for detecting catechol was successfully developed. Since HKUST-1 could fix CDs well and had good catalytic effects on CDs, CDs@HKUST-1/GCE showed stable and excellent ECL intensity. In the presence of lower concentrations of catechol, a high quenching effect on the ECL signal of CDs@HKUST-1/GCE was obtained. The logarithm of catechol concentration and the ECL intensity in quenching ranged from 5.0 × 10−9 to 2.5 × 10−5 mol/L, showing a good linear relationship with a detection limit of 3.8 × 10−9 mol/L (S /N = 3). At the same time, compared with the reported literature, the method was simple, low cost, rapid in testing, and was a safe, efficient and environmentally friendly method for detecting catechol in tea. Featuring favorable performances, the strategy may be rationally designed to serve as a potential practical sensor for food analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of the Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions of China (18KJB150001 and 19KJB150003), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20190928), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51874050 and 21904014) and the Foundation of Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Advanced Catalytic Materials and Technology (BM2012110).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jelechem.2020.114215.
